LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

How to use Easy Apply effectively on LinkedIn

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Manisha S.
10mo Edited
  • Report this post

🚨 Let’s talk about Easy Apply (from someone who reviews CVs and shortlists candidates on a daily basis!) Easy Apply is a one-click job application feature on LinkedIn. Instead of filling out long application forms, you just send your LinkedIn profile (and your CV) straight to the employer. One click, no fuss. You can apply for hundreds of roles within minutes. However... Easy Apply won’t get you far if you’re clicking on every single job or haven't read the job advert. Employers know when: ❌ You don’t meet the basic criteria or aren’t based in the required location - this will likely be an instant rejection ❌ Your CV feels too generic and not tailored to the role ❌ Your profile doesn’t demonstrate any of the key skills they’re looking for ❌ You haven’t shown why you’re a strong fit for the position Here’s how to make Easy Apply actually work for you: 💡 Be selective. Don't just click Easy Apply on everything - read the job spec and criteria. 💡Tailor your CV. A 5-minute tweak to mirror the job description can make a huge difference. Use those key words! 💡Follow up. If you really want the role, reach out to the recruiter or hiring manager after applying and make it personal. 💡Think quality > quantity. In my eyes, 10 strong, relevant applications are worth more than 100 generic ones. Easy Apply isn’t a magic button - it’s a tool. Use it in the right way, and it will get you noticed.

57 5 Comments
Like Comment

  • Report this comment

I agree but since everyone is reaching out to recruiter, your message on linkedin gets burried and never seen by the receiver. Best case scenario, your cv is seen but never responded to . I would love to hear your thoughts on how to overcome this challenge too ?

  • Aneel Kumar
    8mo
    • Report this post

    𝒀𝒐𝒖𝒓 10-𝑺𝒕𝒆𝒑 𝑮𝒖𝒊𝒅𝒆 𝒕𝒐 𝑮𝒆𝒕𝒕𝒊𝒏𝒈 𝑵𝒐𝒕𝒊𝒄𝒆𝒅 (𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝑯𝒊𝒓𝒆𝒅) 1. 𝐁𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐚 𝐃𝐞𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐑𝐨𝐥𝐞 Don't just read the job description once. Study 3 to 5 similar job postings in your field. What keywords keep popping up? Those repeated words aren't just buzzwords—they’re your golden signals for what companies truly value. 2. 𝐃𝐢𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝐭𝐡𝐞 '𝐎𝐧𝐞-𝐒𝐢𝐳𝐞-𝐅𝐢𝐭𝐬-𝐀𝐥𝐥' 𝐑𝐞𝐬𝐮𝐦𝐞 Let's be real: sending the same resume everywhere is lazy. Customize every version. Show the hiring manager that you took the time to understand their specific job and that you're the perfect, tailored fit. 3. 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐖𝐢𝐧𝐬, 𝐃𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐂𝐡𝐨𝐫𝐞𝐬 Nobody cares about your old duties; they care about your impact. Swap vague responsibilities with data-driven achievements. 4. 𝐁𝐞𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐁𝐨𝐭𝐬 (𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐓𝐒 𝐂𝐡𝐞𝐜𝐤) Before a human sees your resume, a robot (the ATS) often scans it. Keep it simple! Use standard, clear headings (Experience, Education, Skills). Avoid fancy graphics, columns, or complex layouts—the bot simply can't read them. 5. 𝐖𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐞 𝐚 𝐊𝐢𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐫 𝐋𝐢𝐧𝐤𝐞𝐝𝐈𝐧 𝐇𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 Your headline is your digital handshake. Don't just list your job title. Combine it with your niche and the value you bring. 6. 𝐂𝐫𝐚𝐟𝐭 𝐚𝐧 𝐄𝐧𝐠𝐚𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐠 '𝐀𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭' 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 This isn't your resume—it's your story. What motivates you? What makes you unique? Use this space to tell your personal career narrative and highlight the difference you make, not just the jobs you've held. 7. 𝐇𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐳𝐞 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐟𝐢𝐥𝐞 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐒𝐨𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐨𝐟 Credibility is king. Add recommendations from former colleagues, feature posts that show your expertise, and get those skill endorsements built up. It's proof that others trust you. 8. 𝐁𝐞 𝐏𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭 (𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐲 𝐖𝐢𝐧𝐬) Recruiters notice the professionals who are consistently visible. Comment, engage, and post at least once a week. Show up, share your insights, and be a regular presence in your industry's conversations. 9. 𝐓𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐭 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡 𝐋𝐢𝐤𝐞 𝐚 𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐣𝐞𝐜𝐭 (𝐓𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐤 𝐈𝐭) A simple spreadsheet or tracker is your best friend. Track your applications and, more importantly, measure your response rates. This feedback loop lets you refine your approach based on what’s actually working. 10. 𝐃𝐨𝐧'𝐭 𝐁𝐞 𝐀𝐟𝐫𝐚𝐢𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐆𝐞𝐭 𝐚𝐧 𝐎𝐮𝐭𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐞 𝐕𝐢𝐞𝐰 It’s tough to spot the flaws in your own work. Sometimes, a professional resume writer or profile specialist can polish the things you're blind to. An investment in help is an investment in your future. Here's the final takeaway: Most job seekers chase quantity. Smart professionals chase quality. If this helps you, please repost it—let's help someone else who's struggling with their job search.

  • Ellie Littlechild
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Here’s the Best Way to Email a Recruiter: I get so many emails every single day from candidates and truthfully, some stand out immediately, while others get lost in the mix. If you’re reaching out to a recruiter (me or anyone else), here are a few tips that really make a difference 👇 1️⃣ Keep Your Subject Line Clear A direct, relevant subject line helps us know what you’re looking for right away. 2️⃣ Personalise the Email A small detail that goes a long way- use our name! “Hi Ellie,” feels a lot more genuine than writing nothing and just sending a CV with no words. 3️⃣ Get to the Point Quickly We read a lot of emails- so short and specific is key. In 2–3 sentences: • Introduce yourself • Mention what type of role you’re applying for • Share one or two standout achievements 4️⃣ Include Your CV Make it easy for us to learn more about your background right away. ✨Following up after a week is totally fine just keep it friendly and professional.

    8mo
    • Report this post

    You might not realize how many top-tier applicants you're really losing because of job descriptions. I often hear that old job posts get recycled and templates are forgotten, leading to language getting watered down. And with only 14 seconds to hook someone, those candidates will keep scrolling. Check out this clip to see how I use Textio to fix the problem using... -Consistent structure and tone across every role and every JD -Real-time feedback on bias, clarity, and compliance -Feedback on templates, not just your final product, so updates get baked into the process If you want to make every job post count, I’ve created a free 10-point checklist so you can evaluate your own job postings. Just drop a comment on this post and I’ll send it over to you.

  • Kehinde Akinwumi Akinyemi
    8mo
    • Report this post

    How to Know Your CV Is the Problem (and Not the “Village People”) 10 Things to Note: 1. You’ve Sent Out Over 50 Applications and Heard Nothing Back If employers never call or email — not even for a rejection — your CV might not be passing the first screening. 2. Your CV Still Starts with “Curriculum Vitae” at the Top Recruiters already know it’s a CV. Use that space for your name and contact details instead. 3. You Use One CV for Every Job Application I am not saying you should make new cv anytime you want to apply for a new job but If you are using the same CV for Customer Care, Administrative Support, Human Resources and Virtual Assistant roles, you are doing yourself a disservice. Each field values different skills and achievements. Have separate, tailored versions of your CV — one for each career path — to match specific job descriptions and keywords. 4. Your Career Objective Sounds Like Everyone Else’s Statements like “To contribute my quota to the growth of an organization” don’t impress recruiters. Use a clear, focused summary that shows what you offer. 5. There Are No Quantifiable Results in Your Experience Employers love numbers. Instead of “Handled customer service tasks,” write “Resolved an average of 20+ customer inquiries daily with 95% satisfaction rate.” 6. Your CV Looks Like a Wall of Text Poor formatting, no bullet points, and no clear sections make it hard to read. Recruiters spend only a few seconds scanning your document. 7. You Have Irrelevant or Outdated Details Remove primary school, local church committees, or unrelated hobbies — they do not add value to your professional profile. 8. Your Email Address Looks Unprofessional A CV sent from cutebabe1997@gmail.com or bossman4life@yahoo.com is a red flag. Use a professional format — e.g. firstname.lastname@gmail.com. 9. Your CV Is Longer Than 2 Pages for an Entry-Level Role Recruiters prefer concise, focused CVs. Include only relevant experiences and achievements. 10. You’ve Never Updated It Since You Graduated A CV should grow with you. New skills, certifications, or roles should be added regularly to reflect your current value.

  • ai-solutions

    8mo
    • Report this post

    🚫 𝗧𝗵𝗲 “𝗔𝗽𝗽𝗹𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝗮𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝗝𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗮𝘀 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗹𝗲” 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝘆 𝗗𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸 I hear this advice all the time: “Just apply everywhere. Something will stick.” The truth is, that approach usually leads to silence, not interviews. 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝘆: 1. 𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻’𝘁 𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗾𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆. Sending the same generic resume to dozens of jobs only creates noise. It is better to send five tailored applications that truly fit than fifty that do not stand out. 2. 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗿𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁. They scan resumes quickly. If your achievements do not speak directly to the company’s needs, your application gets overlooked, even if you are qualified. 3. 𝗖𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗶𝘀𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘄𝗶𝗻𝘀 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲. I have seen clients go from months of no replies to multiple interview requests within weeks simply by tailoring their resumes and cover letters. As a professional resume writer, I help professionals turn “I’ve done X” into “Here’s what I achieved and how it made a difference.” 📈 Example: Instead of saying “Managed reporting processes,” we highlight “Implemented a dashboard that cut month-end close time by 40 percent and freed two analysts for strategic projects.” If you are ready to stop applying blindly and start applying strategically, let’s connect. Comment below or message me with the industry you are targeting and your biggest challenge in your resume or applications. I will share one actionable tip you can use right away.

  • Aminah Bukola
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Your CV Could Be Closing Doors You Don’t Even Know About When I first started applying for jobs, I thought my CV was good enough. It had all the basics: 📌 Job titles. 📌 Responsibilities. 📌 A neat format I downloaded from Google. It looked tidy. It looked professional. But it didn’t work. I sent application after application and all I got was silence. 👉🏾 That’s when I realised: my CV wasn’t the problem. The way I told my story was. I was writing it like a checklist of tasks, not as proof of impact. I was writing it for the jobs I’d already done, not the jobs I wanted next. And if you rush in like that, you rush out just as quickly. ✨ Here are 5 shifts that completely changed the way recruiters saw me (and they can change yours too): 1. Outcomes over tasks. ❌ “Managed Instagram page.” ✅ “Created and scheduled campaigns that grew engagement by 45% in 3 months.” The first tells them what I did. The second proves why it mattered. 2. Write for your future, not just your past. If you’re aiming for Marketing Manager, don’t hide behind words like “assisted.” Highlight the moments you took ownership, led an idea, or coordinated a project. Position yourself as already growing into the role. 3. Speak their language. Recruiters scan for alignment. If the ad says “stakeholder engagement,” and your CV says “worked with people,” you’ll miss the match. Mirror their language — it shows you understand their world. 4. Build a proof bank. I created a simple “evidence folder” on my laptop with: ✔️ Campaign results (e.g. “open rates improved by 10%”) ✔️ Praise emails from managers ✔️ Notes from successful projects That way, updating my CV became easy — I wasn’t relying on memory, I had receipts. 5. Make it skimmable. Recruiters spend less than 10 seconds on a CV. Break it into bullet points. Use action verbs. Add white space. A CV that’s easy to read is a CV that gets read. And here’s the most important part: Nothing about my skills changed. What changed was the way I framed them. Once I stopped listing tasks and started showing results, recruiters didn’t just skim. They started calling. That’s the difference between being overlooked and being considered. So If this post found you tonight, take it as your sign: 👉🏾 Open your CV. 👉🏾 Pick just one line. 👉🏾 Rewrite it as an outcome, not a task. Because your CV shouldn’t just tell your past. It should sell your future. 💬 Over to you: What’s one CV line you know needs rewriting? Drop your “before and after” in the comments 👇🏾 — your rewrite could be the reminder someone else needs tonight. 🧡 #Day68of90 #CareerGrowth #CVTips #JobSearch #Resilience #InternationalStudents

  • ApplyEngine.AI

    63 followers

    9mo
    • Report this post

    Stop letting your resume be a digital doorstop. It needs more than just job titles. Many professionals struggle to stand out in today's crowded job market. Recruiters often skim resumes in seconds. They're looking for candidates who bring more than just technical skills. It's about showing you're a well-rounded individual. The strategy? Inject compelling soft skills directly into your resume's accomplishment statements. This subtly highlights your character. It showcases your ability to thrive in a team and adapt to challenges. It's not just what you did, but how you did it. Here's how: * 🤝 **Reframe your achievements.** Don't just list tasks. Instead, add the soft skill you leveraged to achieve them. For example, instead of "Managed social media accounts," try "Collaboratively managed social media accounts, increasing engagement by 20% through teamwork." This demonstrates leadership and teamwork. * 🗣️ **Quantify your communication skills.** Mention how you effectively conveyed ideas. For instance, "Presented complex data to stakeholders, simplifying it for clear understanding." Then include the positive impact of that communication. This shows you can translate information into action. * 💡 **Showcase problem-solving abilities.** Describe a situation where you overcame a challenge. Something like, "Identified and resolved a critical system error, minimizing downtime by 15%." Focus on the positive impact. This shows initiative and resilience. * ⏱️ **Highlight time management skills.** "Prioritized and completed multiple projects under tight deadlines, resulting in a 10% increase in team productivity." Use metrics to prove efficiency. * 🧠 **Emphasize adaptability.** Describe a time you adjusted to a new situation. For example: “Quickly adapted to a new CRM system, training team members and ensuring a smooth transition with minimal disruption.” Imagine a marketing specialist, Sarah, whose resume simply stated, "Managed email campaigns." She rewrote it to say, "Creatively managed email campaigns, increasing open rates by 25% through targeted content and persuasive messaging." Her interview requests increased by 40% within two weeks. The process only took her an afternoon. A common mistake is listing soft skills without providing evidence. Saying you're a "team player" is not enough. You need to show *how* you've been a team player. Avoid generic descriptions. Think of your resume as a garden. Hard skills are the seeds, but soft skills are the fertilizer. Without them, your potential will never truly bloom. Nurture your soft skills. Make them visible. Watch your career blossom! Ready to transform your resume? Share your biggest resume challenge in the comments below!

  • Ibrahim Akinwunmi, ACA, FMVA®
    8mo

    𝗙𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗼𝘄𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘂𝗽 𝗼𝗻 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗷𝗼𝗯 𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗮 𝘀𝗶𝗻, 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗮 𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝘆. Many people think sending a follow-up email after applying for a job or attending an interview will annoy the HR. No, it won’t. In fact, it might do the exact opposite. A simple, polite follow-up helps the recruiter remember your name. Even if you don’t get that particular job, you’ll likely stay in their mind for future opportunities. Recruiters appreciate proactive and professional candidates who show genuine interest, not those who disappear after hitting “submit.” But please, following up doesn’t mean flooding their inbox with ten emails a week (No oo, Rara oo, mio sobe). Do it with sense. One thoughtful message is enough to show interest without crossing the line. Remember, the goal is not just to get a job, but to build a connection that could open doors later. Click the link below to join the group, where I share daily job offers and practical job search tips. Let’s help you get noticed, not forgotten. https://lnkd.in/dKdQMpqy And if you need a 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗧𝗦-𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗩 that recruiters can’t ignore, send a DM via WhatsApp at https://lnkd.in/dzTVc_G3 or call +2348165051682.

  • Keatlegile Mabena
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Key tips to consider when sending a job application: 👇👇👇 1. Tailor your application • Read the job description carefully and customize your CV and cover letter for that specific role. • Use keywords from the advert to show alignment with the required skills and experience. • Emphasize achievements that match what the employer is looking for. 2. Write a compelling cover letter • Address it to the right person (use the recruiter or hiring manager’s name if possible). • Keep it concise, confident, and specific. One page is ideal. • Explain why you’re interested in the role and how you can add value to the organization. 3. Use a professional email • Use a clear subject line, e.g., Application for Senior Researcher Position – Keatlegile Mabena. • Keep your email body polite and brief, summarizing your intent and attached documents. • Ensure your email address looks professional (avoid nicknames or slang). 4. Attach documents correctly • Include all requested documents (CV, cover letter, qualifications, ID, etc.). • Name your files professionally, e.g., Keatlegile_Mabena_CV.pdf. • Attach files in PDF format to preserve formatting. 5. Respect deadlines and instructions • Submit before the closing date. • Follow all application instructions exactly (e.g., “submit via portal” or “attach all documents in one PDF”). • Avoid sending incomplete applications, it reflects poorly on attention to detail. 6. Proofread everything • Double-check for grammar, spelling, and formatting errors. • Ensure your contact details are correct. • Ask a trusted friend or mentor to review your CV and cover letter. 7. Be professional and polite • Use formal language and a respectful tone. • End your email with a polite note, such as: “Thank you for considering my application. I look forward to the opportunity to contribute to your team.” 8. Follow up (smartly) • If you have not heard back after 7–10 working days, you can send a courteous follow-up email asking for an update.
